questions.question: In which specific anatomical location is the cerebellum situated within the skull?
questions.hint: Think about which part of the skull houses structures of the hindbrain.

questions.question: Which structure connects the two cerebellar hemispheres along the midline?
questions.hint: Its name is derived from the Latin word for 'worm'.

questions.question: Which cell type's axons form the only efferent output from the cerebellar cortex?
questions.hint: These are among the largest neurons in the brain, named after a Czech anatomist.

questions.question: What is the physiological effect of Purkinje cell synapses on the deep cerebellar nuclei?
Which evolutionary zone of the cerebellum is responsible for maintaining body equilibrium and posture?
questions.hint: This is the oldest part of the cerebellum phylogenetically, also known as the flocculonodular lobe.

questions.question: Which cerebellar division acts as a 'Comparator' to correct discrepancies between intended and actual movement?
questions.hint: This division receives proprioceptive input via spinocerebellar tracts.

questions.question: What is the term for the inability to perform rapid, alternating movements?
questions.hint: This clinical sign is tested by asking the patient to rapidly pronate and supinate their hand.

questions.question: How do intention tremors differ from resting tremors?
options: They occur at rest and disappear during movement
They are absent at rest and appear during voluntary activity
They are constant regardless of activity
questions.hint: Think about when cerebellar symptoms typically manifest — at rest or during action?

questions.question: Which deep cerebellar nucleus is the largest and most lateral?
questions.hint: This nucleus is the main output pathway of the neocerebellum.

questions.question: What is the core functional takeaway regarding the cerebellum's role in movement?
options: It does not initiate movement; it perfects it
It is responsible for sensory processing only
questions.hint: The cerebellum is often described as a 'fine-tuner' of motor activity.
